chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; that is the first app-level frame below the allocator/RTL. We attempted to decompile byte_array_to_bson_string and bson_object_from_bytes_len. byte_array_to_bson_string decompiles as a thin wrapper calling through a function pointer PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0(); bson_object_from_bytes_len similarly just calls through PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38(). So the actual implementation (including the memmove call) is via function pointers, not directly visible from the decompilation.

We obtained xrefs to byte_array_to_bson_string: one important caller is bson_object_from_bytes_len at 0x00104348, and also byte_array_to_bson_string itself at 0x00102cec (computed call). The memmove symbol is not directly present (likely inlined or imported via the C runtime), so we cannot decompile it by name. However, the crash reason "Process Termination: memmove" strongly indicates that memmove was invoked with invalid arguments (e.g., OOB src/dst or oversized length), so the potential vulnerability sits in the code that computes the pointers/lengths passed to memmove inside the hidden implementation behind PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 or PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38.

We next examined the JNI entry point Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes, which allocates a 0x28-byte structure, retrieves a pointer and length from the Java byte[] argument via JNI function pointers at *param1+0x5c0 and *param1+0x558, and calls bson_object_from_bytes_len(__ptr,uVar2,(long)iVar1). It then calls a release function at *param1+0x600 to release the Java array elements and checks if the return value lVar3 equals iVar1; if not, it treats this as an unexpected length and frees __ptr, returning -1 (0xffffffffffffffff).

Thus, the length parameter passed into bson_object_from_bytes_len is exactly the Java array length (iVar1) supplied by the caller. There is no further validation or limit here; the function is trusted to handle any provided length up to Java’s array size (which can be large but is bounded by the VM). The crash in memmove therefore must arise from how bson_object_from_bytes_len and/or byte_array_to_bson_string use this length and the data. They likely parse BSON headers and then use memmove to copy internal strings or objects based on lengths inside the BSON document; if these internal lengths are not validated against the outer buffer length, memmove can overread or overwrite.

Critically, the attacker controls the contents of the Java byte array entirely (fuzz_one_input passes arbitrary input to the JNI layer). The JNI glue passes a pointer to the raw bytes and the buffer length down to bson_object_from_bytes_len. If bson_object_from_bytes_len or its helper byte_array_to_bson_string computes offsets/lengths directly from untrusted BSON data and calls memmove without adequate bounds checks, that is a classical out-of-bounds read/write vulnerability (CWE-787/788). The presence of a memmove crash while processing attacker-controlled bytes strongly supports this. There is no evidence of defensive validation between JNI and the memmove implementation; the only post-condition check compares parsed length to original input length and aborts if they differ, but by that point memmove has already been called and may have crashed.

We are missing direct decompilation of the actual implementations behind PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 and PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38, so we cannot show the exact memmove call site or confirm whether the overflow is read-only or write-based. However, given: (1) a C library doing BSON parsing, (2) a memmove crash, (3) fully attacker-controlled byte input, and (4) no visible bounds checks prior to the parsing helpers, it is highly likely that there is a genuine memory safety bug in the BSON parsing code rather than a harness artifact (the JNI code uses standard GetByteArrayElements/ReleaseByteArrayElements pattern and passes correct length). Therefore this should be treated as a real, potentially exploitable vulnerability (likely high severity).
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove while processing attacker-controlled BSON bytes from Java byte[] input
JNI bridge passes raw pointer and unvalidated length directly into bson_object_from_bytes_len and its helper byte_array_to_bson_string

Implementations of bson_object_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string are hidden behind function pointers, but library role (BSON parsing) plus memmove crash strongly indicate an out-of-bounds memory access based on malformed lengths inside the BSON data
recommendations: Decompile or otherwise resolve the function-pointer implementations behind PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38 and PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 to locate the exact memmove call and add strict bounds checks on all lengths/offsets derived from BSON data
Add robust validation of BSON lengths and types before copying or moving memory, ensuring all computed ranges stay within the original buffer length
Augment fuzzing with ASan/UBSan builds of libbson-c-lib.so and capture multiple crashing inputs to characterize and patch all similar parsing bugs
assumptions: bson_object_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string read variable-length fields from the supplied buffer using sizes encoded in the BSON data
JNI helper functions at *param1+0x5c0 and *param1+0x558 are standard JNI GetByteArrayElements and GetArrayLength or equivalents
The crash is not caused by an external allocator or unrelated harness misuse
limitations: Actual memmove call site not directly visible due to indirection via function pointers
Cannot currently confirm whether the overflow is out-of-bounds read vs write or its exact exploitability
No direct view of libbson-c-lib.so’s import table or runtime configuration, which could affect how memmove is linked and used

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove, below it is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, which is the first app-level frame.
Decompilation of byte_array_to_bson_string shows it is a thunk that calls through a function pointer PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0.

Because it is an indirect call, I must try to resolve the implementation via xrefs to the function pointer, but I was not given that symbol’s address/xrefs in the provided tools output; only the thunk itself is visible.

Similarly, bson_array_from_bytes_len and bson_object_from_bytes_len are also pure pointer thunks, so the actual logic, including any memmove invocation, lives in external implementation code pointed to by those function pointers.

Given the stack, memmove is being called from that external implementation (likely part of the same lib, but not decompiled here), so I can’t see the exact src/dst/len expressions or any validation in the real implementation.

However, this is a BSON parser/encoder routine taking a raw byte[] from Java (via JNI) and turning it into internal BSON objects/arrays/strings; fuzzing that input triggered a memmove crash, which strongly suggests an out-of-bounds access driven by the attacker-controlled byte array length/contents.

Because this is not a harness-only null-pointer situation but a standard parser taking untrusted bytes, and because memmove is crashing, the plausible cause is improper bounds checking or length computation inside the BSON-from-bytes routines (classic parsing bug).

There is no indication that the harness is doing anything unrealistic (just passing Java byte[] directly). The code clearly intends to support arbitrary BSON data from Java, so malformed inputs should be handled safely rather than causing memmove to fault.
Indirect-call thunks don’t alter arguments; they simply forward them, so user control of buffer and length is preserved into the unseen implementation, increasing likelihood of a real bug.

Without visibility into the implementation I can’t prove the exact CWE or confirm exploitability, but combining memmove crash, parser context, and attacker-controlled byte[] source, this is very likely a genuine memory-safety vulnerability, at least as a denial-of-service and possibly as more serious corruption depending on where memmove writes.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove invoked while parsing attacker-controlled BSON bytes from Java
Application-level functions are thin thunks to external implementations, so arguments derived from untrusted byte[] remain unchecked at the point of failure
Fuzz harness realistically mimics production JNI usage (passing a Java byte array), not an artificial harness-only misuse
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes -> bson_object_from_bytes_len (thunk) -> PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38 -> bson_array_from_bytes_len (thunk) -> PTR_bson_array_from_bytes_len_00109e18 -> byte_array_to_bson_string (thunk) -> PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 -> memmove
recommendations: Locate and decompile the concrete implementations behind PTR_*_from_bytes_len and PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string, then audit all memmove/memcpy calls for proper bounds checking against the actual buffer sizes
Add strict length and structure validation when parsing BSON from untrusted byte arrays, rejecting or truncating invalid lengths before any memmove/memcpy
Create regression fuzz tests around bson_object_from_bytes_len/bson_array_from_bytes_len with ASan/UBSan to confirm and reproduce the crash and validate the fix
assumptions: Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes exposes BSON parsing to untrusted input in real use, not only in the fuzzer
The memmove crash is due to out-of-bounds or overlapping regions rather than an allocator or external library bug
limitations: Could not decompile the actual implementations behind the function pointers PTR_* so the precise faulty operation is inferred, not directly observed
Stack trace does not include register/argument state for memmove, limiting exact root-cause determination
